### Rhoswen — 2012-04-13T08:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-14624>

For me using coconut oil on my face keeps it looking super nice. I still have some acne, so I like to mix a little tea tree oil and a little coconut oil together in my hands at bed time after I wash my face. I wake up in the morning and my skin just looks so nice!

I'm a very pale, like a ghost, and a red head. I found that when I eat clean, I rarely burn in the sun ever even with no sunscreen. In fact, last summer when I stuck to the Paleo lifestyle, I actually got a tan lines on my feet and arms, even though most people still thought I was ghostly pale, haha! My mom thought the tan lines on my feet were hysterical cause it really did make my toes look super white, like porcelain.

I like to use an all natural cleanser on my face that I got from an Etsy seller right [here](http://http://www.etsy.com/listing/89832398/balance-organic-foaming-face-wash-all) and I use [this](http://http://www.etsy.com/listing/62300993/botanical-hydrating-toner-organic-facial) toner. The cleanser smells DIVINE. When I use those with my coconut/tea tree oil combo, my skin looks great! Could probably skip the tea tree oil if you don't have any acne problems.

### ChristineKleiber — 2012-04-16T14:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-15540>

Granted I am 47, but I just had my second Moh's surgery in January to remove Basal Cell Carcinoma from my head. This is the most common form of skin cancer and given my age, I wanted to know if I could do BHRT or not with this type of cancer history. It turns out that estrogen is protective against BCC - although NOT true for other skin cancers. That would explain as my hormonal cascade was plummeting that voila I now have BCC. FWIW, I did NOT go BHRT as of yet. Instead I got serious about my Paleo/Primal and started the Leptin Rx with chronic low vit D as my major motivator.

On the vanity end of things... well I have a mini Mohawk growing where the 15 staples and skin graft put my head back together. I do use La Roche Posay's Mela -D for my face and also Le Roc's sensitive versions of Retinol about 50% of the time. I started those last Aug and that has aided some, but the biggest change came with the Leptin Rx - skin everywhere is better - except I think it is die off that is causing some erruptive eczema on my knuckles.

BCC doesn't usually kill, but it does disfigure. And watch the legs. For women the more serious skin cancers start there. So before shedding the sunscreens entirely, think how close you are to optimal and make sure you have your estrogen rocking its protective self.

### chocolate — 2012-05-16T22:32:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-24678>

[http://www.naturalnews.com/025302\_skin\_disease\_piperine.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/025302_skin_disease_piperine.html)

Skin Pigment Disease Reversed with Piperine Nutrient From Black Pepper

Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/025302\_skin\_disease\_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xE9I00](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/025302_skin_disease_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xE9I00)

An extract derived from black pepper may be able to help reverse the effects of the skin pigmentation disease known as vitiligo, according to research conducted by scientists from Kings College London and published in the Ridge Journal of Dermatology.

Vitiligo is a disease in which sections of the skin lose their pigmentation and turn white, often leading to a blotchy, mottled look. Because melanin, the chemical that gives skin its pigmentation, also protects against the ultraviolet radiation causes skin cancer, people with vitiligo may be at increased risk for that disease.

The current study focused on piperine, the chemical that gives black pepper its spiciness. Researchers applied piperine and its synthetic derivatives to the skin of mice, and found that doing so caused the animals' skin to turn an even light brown after only six weeks.

When combined with ultraviolet radiation treatment, piperine caused the mice's skin to become darker faster. The effect was also more long-lasting

Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/025302\_skin\_disease\_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xUaGHw](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/025302_skin_disease_piperine.html#ixzz1v3xUaGHw)

### chocolate — 2012-05-20T19:12:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-25740>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430)

Photodamage of the skin: protection and reversal with topical antioxidants.

Burke KE.

Source

[[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

Abstract

Controversy exists as to whether topical antioxidants can be effective in protecting against and reversing photodamage to the skin. Topical vitamins C and E, as well as topical selenium, protect skin against sunburn, suntan and skin cancer and **also reverse the mottled pigmentation **and wrinkles of photoageing**. However, only certain forms of these labile antioxidants are stable and active after percutaneous absorption. For effective topical application, vitamin C must be non-esterified, acidic and optimally at 20% concentration; vitamin E must be the non-esterified isomer d-alpha-tocopherol at 2-5% concentration. Selenium is only percutaneously absorbed and active when applied topically as l-selenomethionine,** optimally at 0.02-0.05%. There are two great advantages in applying an active formulation of topical antioxidants to the skin. First, the skin attains far higher levels of each antioxidant than can be achieved by only taking these vitamins orally. The level of vitamin C attained in the skin by topical application is 20-40 times that achievable with oral vitamin C. With topical application, the concentration of vitamin E in the skin increases by a factor of 10.6 and selenium by a factor of 1.7. Second, topical application arms the skin with a reservoir of antioxidants that cannot be washed or rubbed off, a protection which stays in the skin for several days after application.

You can buy knock off knox gelatin and play around with making your own peels. It just paints on and showers off. It bondos your wrinkles also. Saran wrap is you friend. Whether its circadian or not.

Professional strength, I think you are better off at home than a salon also... You can jump in the shower at home and learn your tolerance.

### chocolate — 2012-08-14T03:03:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-44807>

UC Davis researchers identify cellular basis for how anti-aging costmetics work

> UC Davis researchers identify cellular basis for how anti-aging costmetics work
>
> Published: Monday, August 13, 2012 - 16:05 in Biology & Nature
>
>
>
> A team of investigators from UC Davis and Peking University have discovered a mechanism that may explain how alpha hydroxyl acids (AHAs) ― the key ingredient in cosmetic chemical peels and wrinkle-reducing creams ― work to enhance skin appearance. An understanding of the underlying process may lead to better cosmetic formulations as well as have medical applications. The findings were published in the Journal of Biological Chemistry in an article entitled "Intracellular proton-mediated activation of TRPV3 channels accounts for exfoliation effect of alpha hydroxyl acids on keratinocytes."
>
>
>
> AHAs are a group of weak acids typically derived from natural sources such as sugar cane, sour milk, apples and citrus that are well known in the cosmetics industry for their ability to enhance the appearance and texture of skin. Before this research, little was known about how AHAs actually caused skin to flake off and expose fresh, underlying skin.
>
>
>
> The cellular pathway the research team studied focuses on an ion channel ― known as transient receptor potential vanilloid 3 (TRPV3) ― located in the cell membrane of keratinocytes, the predominant cell type in the outer layer of skin. The channel is known from other studies to play an important role in normal skin physiology and temperature sensitivity.
>
> In a series of experiments that involved recording electrical currents across cultured cells exposed to AHAs, the investigators developed a model that describes how glycolic acid (the smallest and most biologically available AHA) enters into keratinocytes and generates free protons, creating acidic conditions within the cell. The low pH strongly activates the TRPV3 ion channel, opening it and allowing calcium ions to flow into the cell. Because more protons also enter through the open TRPV3 channel, the process feeds on itself. The resulting calcium ion overload in the cell leads to its death and skin exfoliation.
>
>
>
> "Our experiments are the first to show that the TRPV3 ion channel is likely to be the target of the most effective skin enhancer in the cosmetics industry," said Jie Zheng, professor of physiology and membrane biology at UC Davis and one of the principal investigators of the study. "Although AHAs have been used for years, no one until now understood their likely mechanism of action."
>
>
>
> Besides being found in skin cells, TRPV3 also is found in cells in many areas of the nervous system and is sensitive to temperature as well as acidity. The authors speculate that the channel may have a variety of important physiological functions, including pain control.
>
>
>
> Lead author Xu Cao, who conducted the study with UC Davis scientists as a visiting student from Peking University Health Science Center, focuses on TRPV3 channel research. With a team of researchers in China, he recently contributed to the discovery that a mutation in TRPV3 leads to Olmsted syndrome, a rare congenital disorder characterized by severe itching and horny skin development over the palms of the hands and soles of the feet. While in the UC Davis Department of Physiology and Membrane Biology, Cao discovered that AHAs also utilize the TRPV3 channel.
>
>
>
> "**Calcium channels are becoming increasingly recognized as having vital functions in skin physiology," said Cao. "TRPV3 has the potential to become an important target not only for the cosmetics industry but for analgesia and treating skin disease**."

I use this stuff every now and again. Makes you really understand the consequences of eating fruit.

### PrimalPam — 2012-08-14T16:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-44897>

I've used every anti-aging cream on the market, including AHA's. I even met with a derm who wanted to do aggressive laser treatments on me. I read about the potential hazards and cancelled my appointment.

Funny thing is, I started Paleo and then epi-Paleo eating and people keep telling me how much better my skin looks.

In the past few weeks alone, 3 people have thought I was in my mid-30's (I am 50). I went to an ER for what I thought was a fish bone in my throat (gotta love epi-Paleo!). The ER doc called an ENT to get me a scope; I was in the other room, but I heard the ER doc describe me to the ENT doc as a "Female in her mid-30's".

I can't help wondering if all the anti-aging creams are worth it, and if they can actually harm.

### chocolate — 2012-08-30T05:27:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-48296>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/29/bacterial.cause.found.skin.condition.rosacea?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/29/bacterial.cause.found.skin.condition.rosacea?)

Bacterial cause found for skin condition rosacea

Published: Wednesday, August 29, 2012 - 19:31 in Health & Medicine

> Scientists are closer to establishing a definitive bacterial cause for the skin condition rosacea. This will allow more targeted, effective treatments to be developed for sufferers, according to a review published in the Journal of Medical Microbiology. Rosacea is a common dermatological condition that causes reddening and inflammation of the skin mostly around the cheeks, nose and chin. In severe cases skin lesions may form and lead to disfigurement. Rosacea affects around 3% of the population -- usually fair-skinned females aged 30-50 and particularly those with weak immune systems. The condition is treated with a variety of antibiotics, even though there has never been a well-established bacterial cause.
>
>
>
> A new review carried out by the National University of Ireland concludes that rosacea may be triggered by bacteria that live within tiny mites that reside in the skin.
>
>
>
> The mite species Demodex folliculorum is worm-like in shape and usually lives harmlessly inside the pilosebaceous unit which surrounds hair follicles of the face. They are normal inhabitants of the face and increase in number with age and skin damage -- for example, following exposure to sunlight. The numbers of Demodex mites living in the skin of rosacea patients is higher than in normal individuals, which has previously suggested a possible role for the mites in initiating the condition.
>
>
>
> More recently, the bacterium Bacillus oleronius was isolated from inside a Demodex mite and was found to produce molecules provoking an immune reaction in rosacea patients. Other studies have shown patients with varying types of rosacea react to the molecules produced by this bacterium -- exposing it as a likely trigger for the condition. What's more, this bacterium is sensitive to the antibiotics used to treat rosacea.
>
>
>
> Dr Kevin Kavanagh who conducted the review explained, "The bacteria live in the digestive tracts of Demodex mites found on the face, in a mutually beneficial relationship. When the mites die, the bacteria are released and leak into surrounding skin tissues -- triggering tissue degradation and inflammation."

### chocolate — 2012-10-02T03:47:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-55745>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/10/01/marine.animals.could.hold.key.looking.young?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/10/01/marine.animals.could.hold.key.looking.young?)

> Marine animals could hold the key to looking young
>
> Published: Monday, October 1, 2012 - 15:05 in Biology & Nature
>
>
>
> Sea cucumbers and sea urchins are able to change the elasticity of collagen within their bodies, and could hold the key to maintaining a youthful appearance, according to scientists at Queen Mary, University of London. The researchers investigated the genes of marine creatures such as sea urchins and sea cucumbers, known as echinoderms. They found the genes for "messenger molecules" known as peptides, which are released by cells and tell other cells in their bodies what to do.
>
>
>
> The study was published online in the journals PLOS ONE and General and Comparative Endocrinology.

### Jamie S — 2020-01-10T22:45:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284397>

> [chocolate said:](/goto/post?id=25740)
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17134430)
>
> Photodamage of the skin: protection and reversal with topical antioxidants.
>
> Burke KE.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Controversy exists as to whether topical antioxidants can be effective in protecting against and reversing photodamage to the skin. Topical vitamins C and E, as well as topical selenium, protect skin against sunburn, suntan and skin cancer and **also reverse the mottled pigmentation **and wrinkles of photoageing**. However, only certain forms of these labile antioxidants are stable and active after percutaneous absorption. For effective topical application, vitamin C must be non-esterified, acidic and optimally at 20% concentration; vitamin E must be the non-esterified isomer d-alpha-tocopherol at 2-5% concentration. Selenium is only percutaneously absorbed and active when applied topically as l-selenomethionine,** optimally at 0.02-0.05%. There are two great advantages in applying an active formulation of topical antioxidants to the skin. First, the skin attains far higher levels of each antioxidant than can be achieved by only taking these vitamins orally. The level of vitamin C attained in the skin by topical application is 20-40 times that achievable with oral vitamin C. With topical application, the concentration of vitamin E in the skin increases by a factor of 10.6 and selenium by a factor of 1.7. Second, topical application arms the skin with a reservoir of antioxidants that cannot be washed or rubbed off, a protection which stays in the skin for several days after application.
>
>
>

Persevere on your skin beauty quest-- you will find answers that work for you if you leave no stone unturned.

As a man I'm not interested in cosmetic coverups, but I surely don't want to look prematurely old with wrinkles and hyperpigmented skin lesions, etc.

I believe skin health comes most authentically from the inside out. So, besides a relatively clean diet and supplements, I ingest a cocktail consisting of MSM, BioSil, and marine collagen almost daily. It helps keep skin, ligaments, and other connective tissue plump, healthy, and youthful.

Now there is an exciting Ayurvedic natural substance growing in popularity for the anti-aging crowd.

It is **Bakuchiol** oil from plant _Psoralea Corylifolia._ Touted as a safer alternative to retinol it is highly reputed with abilities to reduce hyperpigmentation, inflammation, wrinkles (fine lines), and photoaging, and also boosts elastin and collagen production.

May you look more beautiful and youthful as you grow older. We health-aware guys are counting on you gals to keep up with us!

Yes, Mr Asprey had a paragraph about Bakuchiol in Chapter 13 of his book [Superhuman.](https://www.amazon.com/Super-Human-Bulletproof-Backward-Forever/dp/0062882821/ref=sr_1_1?keywords=superhuman&qid=1578930391&s=books&sr=1-1)

In that chapter he discusses quite a few therapies for aging skin and graying hair. Some of his ideas sound practical to me, others not so practical (e.g. **cryotherapy**, where one strands in air temperature of **\-270 degrees F** for up to 3 minutes).

I currently use CollagenUP brand, which is sourced from enzymatically hydrolized fish collagen peptides, with added Vit C and hyaluronic acid. Its taste is relatively benign and I prefer it to beef collagen which tastes quite yucky 'gamey' to me.

You might want to try a beauty hack I affectionately called **Inger's Protocol**, which is alternating cold water face baths with red light exposures.

Good luck.

> In that chapter he discusses quite a few therapies for aging skin and graying hair. Some of his ideas sound practical to me, others not so practical (e.g. **cryotherapy**, where one strands in air temperature of **\-270 degrees F** for up to 3 minutes).
>
> I currently use CollagenUP brand, which is sourced from enzymatically hydrolized fish collagen peptides, with added Vit C and hyaluronic acid. Its taste is relatively benign and I prefer it to beef collagen which tastes quite yucky 'gamey' to me.
>
> You might want to try a beauty hack I affectionately called **Inger's Protocol**, which is alternating cold water face baths with red light exposures.
>
> Good luck.

Paul Newman supposedly stayed lookin young because of ct on his face

### Inger — 2020-01-15T11:33:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284616>

I watched a older lady as waiting to pay my groceries.. she was maybe around 70 yo, had almost zero wrinkles, but was also pale. Like Paul Newman above  She was not healthy at all, had trouble walking, overweight etc.
I had to think about this conversation... and how I have seen so many older people with almost no wrinkles, but all very unhealthy.
Then I know very wrinkly people, who are very healthy.

I am going to stop thinking wrinkles are bad.

I am still going to do my beauty routine though, I am happy if it helps. But to avoid the sun to avoid wrinkles is insanity to me.
Also, when you have lots of scars and wrinkles... man you are a WARRIOR. Yeah. Life was hard with you, but you survived and came out better of it. Hell yes, maybe we should be proud of our wrinkles instead
I know, it is hard. All media is so anti-wrinkle!

Christine, I have some gray hairs too...lol Not going to color my hair that is for sure.

### Christine_L — 2020-01-15T19:00:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284648>

She used to sun tan in a tanning booth everyday but now she does not. My other coworker is in her 40s with no wrinkles at all. She eats fat and is overweight. They are both very pale. I think the first girl has more wrinkles due to not eating fat and because she avoids the sun now. But what do I know! lol

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-01-15T19:23:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284650>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=284616)

> I know, it is hard. All media is so anti-wrinkle!
>
> Christine, I have some gray hairs too...lol Not going to color my hair that is for sure.

Inger, I'm glad you are comfortable in your own skin, and have made peace with signs of aging. That is a healthy attitude.

Of course we all have our own experiences to draw on with regard to perceptions of people's aging (and I'm guilty like many of [confirmation bias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confirmation_bias) in this).

However, generally I conclude that most people look their age or are even worse for wear in this toxic and stressful modern age. That is, in contrast to your older lady's appearance, they have pale and weathered skin with noticeable wrinkles. To complete the average senior's appearance (> 70 years for exam.), they often look a bit frail and stiff and walk with some discomfort.

Of course like everything in nature there just have to be big exceptions to the rule (keeps things interesting!). Who hasn't read or heard about very old people who are still active and healthy and yet who claim for example that they have smoked a pack of cigarettes and drunk a bottle of scotch every day of their life? **Good genes are priceless**.

Now, it is known that face aging characteristics are somewhat associated with ethnicity. For example, _European_ women can tend to develop lots of fine lines as they age, while _slavic_ types are known more for heavy facial folds and creases rather than fine lines. Interestingly, I have heard that **Catholic nuns** as a group tend to resist skin aging better and have longer longevity than other women. I doubt they are sun worshippers who strip daily to soak in sunlight outside in convent gardens, but I do think it has a lot to do with their healthy and active lifestyle and their calm spiritual attitudes. On the other hand, who hasn't seen pictures of modern cowboys with deeply carved and leathered skin from a lifetime in the saddle on the range?

What is indisputable is that sun UV exposure is associated with up to 80% of skin aging (_sunsafe rx_). I know a lot of Krusians will howl with disapproval at this, and that is okay. It should be noted that Kruse tribe members are doing a lot of other things to help mitigate and minimize resulting skin damage. As a side note, since UV rays are more intense at high altitudes, you get a generous UV dose from rays coming through aircraft windows (flight attendants have an increased risk of melanoma from this, according an analysis of studies in May-June 2006 issue of the _Journal of Travel Medicine).
_
So, all things being equal there seem to be two main variables (besides genes, stress, and diet) involved in accelerated skin aging-- _smoking and sun exposure_. And according to one study, your number of wrinkles is significantly related to your total hours of lifetime sun exposure (_Sunsafe RX_)

Nonetheless, the tradeoff (for some Krusians with greater sun exposure which may mean quickened photo aging) in exchange for better overall health seems to be a good one. Go for it.

### drezy — 2020-01-15T20:18:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284654>

> [ElectricUniverse said:](/goto/post?id=284650)
>
> So, all things being equal there seem to be two main variables (besides genes, stress, and diet) involved in accelerated skin aging-- _smoking and sun exposure_. And according to one study, your number of wrinkles is significantly related to your total hours of lifetime sun exposure (_Sunsafe RX_)
>
> Nonetheless, the tradeoff (for some Krusians with greater sun exposure which may mean quickened photo aging) in exchange for better overall health seems to be a good one. Go for it.

I think you have to look deeper into studies like those and sort out so many factors. Also understand what has to happen in skin cells prior to being damaged. I don't think the "quickened" will be as fast as some might think. There are many factors involved.

From: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/)
"Alpha melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). Upon exposure to sunshine, melanocytes and keratinocytes in the skin release α-MSH, which has been implicated in immunologic tolerance and suppression of contact hypersensitivity. α-MSH also helps limit oxidative DNA damage resulting from UVR and increases gene repair, thus reducing melanoma risk, as reported 15 May 2005 in _Cancer Research_."

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-01-17T18:10:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284732>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=284654)
>
> I think you have to look deeper into studies like those and sort out so many factors. Also understand what has to happen in skin cells prior to being damaged. I don't think the "quickened" will be as fast as some might think. There are many factors involved.
>
>
> From: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2290997/)
> "Alpha melanocyte-stimulating hormone (α-MSH). Upon exposure to sunshine, melanocytes and keratinocytes in the skin release α-MSH, which has been implicated in immunologic tolerance and suppression of contact hypersensitivity. α-MSH also helps limit oxidative DNA damage resulting from UVR and increases gene repair, thus reducing melanoma risk, as reported 15 May 2005 in _Cancer Research_."

With what little we definitely know now about aging, it is not certain about all factors that are in play with photo aging, given an individual's lifestyle and health practices. That is why I say only time will tell about outcomes from Black Swan's propensity to high levels of UV/sunlight exposure.

If we are doing things right with Dr Kruse's optimal health strategies most us would have potential to live a long time-- far longer than modern average lifespan. Whether we would relish living to a healthy 100 years but wrinkled as a prune is up to the individual.

The game changer is if a complete skin renewal/rejuvenating method were invented, possibly with stem cells. Not sure I would want to be the first guinea pig going there, however.

What if that process went terribly awry, and I ended up looking like this below picture. Would women still hit on me?

I like anecdotal examples, but unless we get at the whole truth, and nothing but the truth, then absolute cause and effect is difficult to obtain.

As they have said for ages, beauty is only skin deep.

Of course, as a red-blooded male I am bewitched by all that feminine youthful beauty out there. But I am not fooled by it.

Behind a lot of pretty faces there is often just a void when it comes to moral character, kind and good personality, and intellect. Physical beauty seems to hold out the promise of happiness in the eye of a beholder, but if 'truth in advertising' laws applied here it would a field day for attorneys.

Have you noticed that some women seem to look even more beautiful as they age, even though their skin shows a lot mileage from being 'lived in'? That inner glow comes from a good soul on the inside.

### crix — 2020-01-23T15:35:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284953>

This is likely old news to everyone here, but many
conventional dermatologists and plastic surgery clinics now use lasers (such as fractional CO2 devices) to remove signs of aging in the skin. This video is from the famous Mayo
Clinic <

\>.

Here is more information from "Lasers and IPL Techniques in Dermotology and Aesthetics", edited by Christian Raulin and Syrus Karsai:

"Fractional skin resurfacing generates improvements in surface texture, wrinkling, and global skin appearance. The treatment is ideally suited to patients with mild to moderate textural changes or rhytids who desire more dramatic results than traditional nonablative lasers may provide but less downtime than traditional ablative therapy. Fractional resurfacing delivers ablative or nonablative laser energy in a pixilated pattern, creating microscopic columns of thermal injury in the epidermis and dermis. These coagulated areas are surrounded by areas of untouched skin, which allow for rapid healing and re-epithelialization. As a result, fractional resurfacing more effectively treats rhytids and surface texture compared with nonablative or skin-tightening techniques and offers decreased down- time and a reduced risk of adverse events relative to ablative laser skin treatment."

Full laser resurfacing of the face can cost $1000, but I have seen used CO2 fractional lasers for sale for as low as $5000-$10000. A group of likeminded people in the same area could get together, buy one, and train themselves how to use it using free material put out by the manufacturer.

### ElectricUniverse — 2020-01-23T18:21:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vanity-and-face-wrinkles.831/post-284965>

> [Novah said:](/goto/post?id=284942)
>
> Agh. I know your intent to write this is good but it comes across all wrong. The pretty girls I know have great character, strong intellect and are nothing but kind; nothing void about them in the least.

My statement obviously wasn't meant to be a generalized blanket condemnation of all pretty girls, and I think I worded it right then and I would say it again.

First of all, pretty faces are a dime a dozen, and I don't find a close correlation between looks and high character.

I know many very attractive, mature women (younger women under say, 35, are generally not mature in many character aspects-- often they are just looking for a good time and material pursuits until the biological clock starts ticking loudly) that are top shelf in almost every refined attribute that would be desirable to discerning men. However, I wouldn't say they are representative of the average female, especially in this [Kali Yuga](https://www.yogapedia.com/definition/5302/kali-yuga) age. That is what makes them so special, and yes indeed, uncommon. Same goes for men in general.

### Owl — 2012-04-15T07:04:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-15243>

> **August;15083 wrote:** I gave iodine supplementation a whirl in hopes that it would help my PCOS (and insulin resistance, which was worse in those days) . . . like a lot of other things that I have tried, I did not notice that it made much if any difference . . I think I worked up to 50 mg/day . . and I think I gave it 4 months . . . maybe that was not enough time . . . . also at the time I was using birth control to manage my PCOS . . and I was taking synthroid and a T-3 from a compounding pharmacy for hypothyroidism . . .. maybe those things interfered with either the iodine itself . . or my ability to perceive the difference made by taking it (birth control masks some of the symptoms which I think can be good and bad) . . . I am now off of those . . and I am only taking progesterone for the 2nd half of my cycle . . .I have thought that maybe I ought to give iodine supplementation another chance (it is controversial . . although I have to admit I sort of like the theory that it might be helpful for PCOS and insulin resistance . . . I also have fibercystic breast issues too . . . it did seem like the iodine was somewhat helpful with that) . . . . maybe iodine + CT . . . maybe plus leptin reset . . . and maybe another round of the HCG Diet for good measure . . . would be worth a whirl . . . although it seems like iodine supplementation can cause too rapid detoxing issues . . I think that might also be true of CT . . . and I think that's also true of the HCG diet . . .now that I think about it . . I think the reason that the iodine supplementation feel by the way side was because I wanted to try the HCG Diet . . and I was afraid to take Iodoral while doing that . . and I just never got back to iodine supplementation . . . it does seem like I have several conditions that might be helped by iodine (using it for thyroid is very controversial) . . . . I've tried lots of different things to try to deal with PCOS . . some of them probably did not get a fair shake re: how much time I gave them . . . .

I think you should try iodine again. If you got up to 50mg in 4 months, you must not have had much detox from it. It sounds like CT causes much more detox than iodine, if you titrate up slowly. And CT won't replace your need for iodine. If you have FBD too, you definitely need it. It's something that you need long term, not for just a few months. It's going to take quite a while to reverse both conditions. And you'll need iodine after that, just maybe not as much.

I lost 75lb on hcg, and didn't have any detox issues. That's not much of a concern on hcg that I can see. I'm also on T3 and Naturethroid. My meds have not changed since I've been on iodine. I'm up to 25mg/day. I did take 200mg of selenium for months before starting iodine. Although I don't have it, that is especially important if you have Hashi's.

August,

I've been on the reset since I finished a round of hcg last September. Since then, I've been low carb, keto-paleo. Added CT in February. I have hashimotos and started t3 around the same time. I also have adrenal fatigue and have been taking Wilson's Adrenal Rebuilder for support. My testosterone is high, p&e ratio is good. I've always been overweight, lots of acne, terrible periods, so I got on HBC. I got off of that in February and can't remember the last time I've cycled. I'm no longer overweight...5'3" and 110 lbs. The doc suspects lupus but wanted to test me for PCOS first.

My basement flooded over the weekend, and I splurged on chocolates and wine while sucking water on Saturday night. Stress eating = bad. I'm wondering if that would skew my Wednesday blood work? Either way, the doc doesn't want to put me on drugs and will retest in 6 months.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-05-10T19:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-22915>

Yeah, I have a lot going on. HCG really was the catalyst that helped me learn so much about my health, but it also unveiled a lot of problems that I think I have had for a long time.

To answer your questions about hcg...my highest weight was 177.7 back in 2007. I started hcg at 146 and after two rounds (couldn't maintain the first round), my LIW was 118. With the leptin reset, keto-paleo style, and avoiding food sensitivities like nightshades, eggs, nuts and dairy, I maintained and stayed at about 117 from Sept.-Feb. Then, in February, I got on t3 for my thyroid and started CT...baths a couple times/week, but now mostly cold showers and spot-CT, and today's weigh-in was 109.4. I dropped very quickly, not sure if it was from the CT or the T3. My BMI shows around 19.5, but I'm not sure body comp. My hormonal issues are what is hurting my reshaping...I still have a tire around my belly, but I have lost a lot of fat in my boobs and arms.

The leptin reset ABSOLUTELY has helped me maintain. I eat the BAB, I limit carbs, and I got tested for food sensitivities. I'm allergic to zucchini, squash, rhubarb, strawberry, bacon (but not pork) and a few other things that I can't remember off the top of my head. With my adrenals, I gave up coffee and chocolate to avoid stimulation from caffeine. All of these things are working very well, and I feel better than ever.

My biggest thing now is getting my cycle back and waking up my adrenals. My huge set-back was a few weeks ago where I got a MAJOR sunburn from being out in the sun a few hours and gained over 15 lbs. overnight. I was completely puffed up, and that's why my doc suspects lupus, which is another AI related to hashis.

Anyway, if you have known sensitivities, I would avoid at all costs. There is no reason to tear up your gut. Many of us struggle with leaky gut, and it will only set your body back further. Eat safe foods, lots of fats and proteins, and you will do great on the reset. Add in CT, and I think you will be unstoppable.

wow -didnt realize all this. I have hashi's and our weights sound similar. i'm on my first hcg round and its the only thing that has worked in getting off the "weird" weight gain i got from my hashi's. i say weird bc i never used to gain in my face and arms and that is where it went. no matter what i did - CT/leptin/lowcarb/paleo/smart exercise, the scale wouldnt budge.

i had/have adrenal fatigue so had a hard time tolerating thyroid meds and so have never been able to get my thyroid level where it needs to go. i was able to increase though recently (after a year of waiting!) and am on a super low dose of hydrocortisone in hopes that it will rest my adrenals (i was on adaptogens but they werent really doing much), and allow me to increase my thyroid meds successfully. my dose of HC is 15mg, spaced out 3 times during the days. hoping its small enough so that i will be be able to taper off within a year without too much hassle and also avoid side effects.

i'm thankful to know more about food sensitivities and leptin reset for when i stop hcg (should have last injection on friday). since i'm still figuring out hormones (just started bio-identicals, progesterone/testosterone -they were both low) i will not be surprised if i need to do another round of hcg to settle. i'm just thankful it's there to leverage as i get a handle on what is going on with my body.

what did you use to determine your food sensitivities? i am going to weight daily and slowly rotate things through to see how they affect me. 15pound weight gain overnight?! wow! how quickly did that come off?

i'm grateful not to have PCOS but know it can happen at anytime so hoping to avoid it in the future by doing what I learn here

I did muscle testing for food sensitivities through my chiropracter. My best advice to you coming off hcg is to follow the leptin rx and stick to keto paleo. Also, go slow at first. Nudge your calories up slowly.

The 15-lb. sunburn gain took a week to come off, that was with sleeping with my feet elevated, CT, drinking aloe vera and taking lots of omega 3s.

yikes - that sounds horrible! your body most have retained/swollen up to help heal the burn. ouch. glad you are doing much better! i hope to be able to continue to tolerate my thyroid meds so i can improve. it must have been amazing to see the pounds come off without hcg - thats truley a sign of healing!

I did the 72 hours, and then I think I went up to 800, then 1000, then 1200, etc. staying at each for a couple of days. I didn't work out, but I'm struggling with extreme fatigue and live on a ranch, so that's my workout. I think walking, biking and yoga would be great, but don't overdo it. One thing I did was pushups and situps daily before I went to sleep. Also, be sure to add veggies in slowly. It took me awhile to figure some things out -- like that tomatoes made me gain -- because I added in several veggies together at once. Just take it slow, drink your water and don't be afraid of salt. I didn't use salt for months when I started because I thought it made me gain....but I think that made my adrenal fatigue worse! Now, I liberally use sea salt on everything.

Good luck!

yes, i'm worried about tomatoes....i am going to experiment with them (on a salad ok? just in ketchup? how about tomato sauce? etc). i know eggplant bothers me, and i'm assuming almost everything in the nightshades family. ill be careful with the veggies. i'm hoping to try fruit too - strawberries and blueberries for once in a blue moon.

shoudl i be careful with things like coconut oil and mac nuts?

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-05-23T16:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-26655>

I found out that I'm sensitive to strawberries, not to say you are, but my chiro said it's a common allergy. I seem to do well with fruit, but note that fructose, without glucose, converts straight to fat. I would also avoid ketchup and tomato sauce, as the sugars of the tomatoes are greater in these products. Stick to fresh. And, my first BAB was steak with cocoa crack (coconut oil, cocoa and stevia), so add lots of CO to your diet; your body will love it! I don't do macadamia nuts and am just now going to try to make something with almonds after being on the reset since last fall, so be careful with nuts. When in doubt, follow autoimmune paleo, and you'll do great.

### 2bls — 2012-05-24T21:45:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-27140>

Ah, PCOS, that ugly demon! I've had it since puberty but daignosed at 26 and now am 43. I did all the standard drugs for it, Met and Spiro and that masked some symptoms so my doctor marked my PCOS as "reversed" in my file and doesn't feel necessary for follow up bloodwork....so, I'm on my own. Just started a clean round of LR(day 9) and am hoping that my Lugols will help to turn things around. Taking most of the buddy supplements for Iodine and don't really notice any bad detox symptoms. I did one salt flush just in case... I can't wait for LR to start its work so that I can notice something...My left eyebrow is growing back so that's one good word for iodine use YAY! I hope my scalp hair will also make a reappearance. I take detox supplements for estrogen dominance detox(d-glucarate and Indol-3-Carbinole) and they seem to help. I haven't Ovulated yet but I'm hopeful as I've been noticing some cervical muscous for the last several months. Not Ovulatory CM but some which I never had before...I think the old quote "time is a great healer" applies here too. When you have your treatment ducks in a row, time is the variable which will make the most difference I think. Consistency and passage of time...those will probably be my keys to success...and great patience.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-05-25T22:58:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-27445>

> **nuttmegs17;27248 wrote:** yes all good points. I have been stuck on ata conference with not the greatest foodchoices asi end HCG so I ended up having a fruit cup (just diced fruit) which is completely out of character for me, but i was starving. i generally feel fruit isnt worth the calories bc i am never full after eating. i think autoimmune paleo will be the best bet as i come off...gonna load up on healthy fats. and then try things one at a time and see how i do.
>

> do you mind sharing a couple "typical days" of food? i'm looking for ideas! i think i'm ready for the coco crack (99% dark choc yum!)

Sure thing. Here's a typical day. Remember, I'm keto paleo, so I limit my carbs...but I will add in caulirice, broccoli, spinach, Libby's pumpkin puree, coconut butter or flakes, lettuce, onions, etc. Usually, I try to keep my veggies at breakfast and lunch. I'm a cattle rancher, so beef is abundant at my house...

Here's a typical day:

Breakfast: 8 oz. ground beef made into meat balls and fried in 1 T coconut oil. I have fresh herbs that I often cut and mix in, or I use curry, turmeric, cinnamon, nutmeg, garlic, salt and pepper. Whatever I feel like...

Lunch: 4 oz. side pork (uncured, unsalted bacon strips basically) seasoned with cinnamon and salt. Try this with pork sometime. It's the bomb!

Supper: 6 oz. venison steak or pork chop with another T of CO.

Usually, I keep lunch in just to support my adrenals. It's usually pretty small. Hope this helps.

Okay, this may be a dumb question, but what's the difference between ovulatory cm and regular cm? I've been noticing some myself, but I don't menstruate and am trying to better understand what my body is doing....

I got off the pill in February and no cycle yet. I'm really, really anxious to get back to normal...but don't know what else to do besides paleo and CT to get on track.

to be honest, I only know that Ovulatory CM is 'supposed' to be high in volume, clear(not opaque or cloudy) with no yellowish colour, and really really stretchy like egg whites when you wipe. This is all theoretical for me since I have never gotten this kind of CM myself. Mine usually is sort of opaque white, increasing a little up to the day I'm supposed to ovulate and then disappearing after that til my period arrives and starts everything over again...sorry I can't be more informative.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-25T23:57:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-27461>

> **PaleoCowgirl;27544 wrote:** Okay, this may be a dumb question, but what's the difference between ovulatory cm and regular cm? I've been noticing some myself, but I don't menstruate and am trying to better understand what my body is doing....
>
>
>
> I got off the pill in February and no cycle yet. I'm really, really anxious to get back to normal...but don't know what else to do besides paleo and CT to get on track.

Think post AF pre ovulation CM to be sticky, tacky, clumpy, and kind of whiteish/yellowish

as you move toward ovulation - it will thin out, and not be as sticky, tacky or clumpy - and will begin to turn clear

at ovulation, it will be the consistency of an egg white - runny, flexible, and clear - If you were to put some on your fingers rub together then pull apart - it would stick to both fingers and stretch this makes it the better to catch sperm with, it allows the sperm to survive longer in the female reproductive tract (sometime upto 5 days after intercourse) and help them get to the egg for fertilization... it is possible to get pregnant w/o a cycle - as long as you have the proper CM pattern (Raises hand!)

after ovulation - it will transition back to sticky, then eventually none right before TOTM.

This is why when charting - CM, Cervix height/opening, and Temps are critical for either planning or preventing pregnancy

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-06T15:36:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-29697>

> **PaleoCowgirl;29769 wrote:** Okay, here's another question...I was reading up on fertility and said if you do ovulate, you will bleed. Is this true? I thought perhaps I was ovulating based on CM, but then again, I haven't seen a menstrual cycle yet or any spotting for that matter.
>
>
>
> Honestly, I'm starting to get really scared. My husband is starting to talk babies, and I will admit, I think I will be ready within the next year or so for one, too. But, I'm worried I won't be able to get pregnant, or if I do, I will miscarry. Between hashis and pcos and not cycling, how screwed am I?

Not true. I've gotten pregnant w/o TOTM. there has been record of young girls who haven't had their cycle getting pregnant - why? they're ovulating before they actually start cycling - their hormones are awake and kicking - the difference is the uterine lining isn't building up. Additionally, when women have lactational ammenehorea - they can still ovulate and get pregnant - so IMO and IME total BS. track your cycles, temp, cm, cervix height/opening. etc. you'll begin to see a pattern. do you have Taking Charge of your fertility? its a great book. FWIW if I can get pregnant and carry 2 separte kids w/o any significant P throughout my cycle. As you continue working on your health things will change, you'll get there. is there a guarantee - no, but you're putting yourself in the best position for success, by changing your Leptin status. continue working on it...things will fall into place, though it may take a while... for the 1st time in my life I have predictable cycles - running like clockwork! initially all from the LR, now w/the BHRT from DzLogic - I'm actually migraine free, and TOTM isn't painful or miserable! I can actually function during TOTM.

Taking Charge is where I thought I read that information, but thanks for clarifying. I keep reading blogs of people with PCOS who were able to return their menses within a few months of going paleo. I've been eating paleo/doing leptin reset since September and no signs of a cycle yet. I keep track of my temps, cm, etc. on fertility friend and am seeing a pattern, but as I up my thyroid meds, my temps go up, too, so it always thinks I'm pregnant because my temps are above my coverline. August,

Owl & mom28 are correct. Listen to what they say. The Dr. Brownstein Iodine Supplementation Program is exactly what you need to successfully treat your PCOS. Lugols or iodoral Iodine is the magic that successfully treats hypothyroidism, Fibrocystic Breast Disease, PCOS, Cysts, & Endometriosis in women. Also successfully treats Prostate BPH in men, iodine cured my own BPH problems. Every cell in our body requires iodine to be healthy, especially true for the thyroid, breasts brain & sex organs. **

August,

First of all, your thyroid requires** more iodine than any other organ in your body. Yes your TSH is expected to rise and that is a normal reaction to giving your SICKLY thyroid its required iodine. As your thyroid slowly heals itself, you will be reducing your thyroid meds. Many people actually end up tossing their thyroid meds out when their thyroid completely heals. For others, the lack of iodine has permanently damaged their thyroids, and they will be required to take thyroid hormone meds for life. In either case, continue taking your iodine with required supplements because every cell in your body requires iodine, especially your breasts to prevent Fibrocystic Breast Disease, your uterus to prevent Uterus Cysts, and your ovaries to treat & prevent future PCOS. Just as important is iodine for your brain to prevent brain fog, depression, memory problems & migraine.

I suggest you follow Dr. Brownstien's supplement list exactly. Each supplement has very important reasons to be on the list. Your muli does not have enough B2 & B3 to be effective. If you want to get the most from your iodine, you MUST follow doctors orders, especially since you have PCOS. After your PCOS is cured, you may want to experiment more. The selenium is ESSENTIAL for your thyroid, I have several excellent articles that explain why, see Iodine References, chapter Selenium. The ATP Cofactors is essential to open pathways for toxins to exit & for iodine to enter your cells. Without the ATP Cofactors, you are wasting money on iodine. This is why iodine was not so effective the last time you tried. So if you want to heal your PCOS, then follow Doctors Brownstein Orders. Doctor knows best.

In regard to your thyroid labs. I want you to read this vital article.

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread45205-256.html#post844257](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread45205-256.html#post844257)

The thyroid drives your metabolism. Thyroid labs are not accurate, they are affected by toxins in your body. Therefore, your thyroid labs are secondary to measuring your basal body temperature. BODY TEMP RULES WHEN IT COMES TO THE THYROID. Use your body temperature as the MAIN guage for taking your thyroid meds. You want to adjust your thyroid meds to stay at 98.6 degrees. As your thyroid heals from taking iodine, you will begin slipping into hyperthyroid, and your body temp will go above 98.6 degrees, and you reduce your thyroid medicine. You may eventually not need any more thyroid meds at all to keep your body temp at 98.6 degrees. My own hypothyroid was cured with iodine. My body temp is now a rock solid 98.6 every morning. I used to suffer from cold fingers & feet. I like my Vicks Brand digital thermometer, gets a large color reading in only 10 seconds. ALSO, at the heart of all of your hormones is the thyroid. Heal your thyroid with iodine and your hormones will naturally improve.

Lastly, it is vital to continue your iodine supplement program. The reason is all the Halogen Toxins attacking our iodine every day of the week. We must take enough iodine to overcome the toxins coming into our body every day. Bromide in bread & everything made with flour. Also Bromide in soft drinks, other foods. Then you have your water treated with Fluoride & Chlorine. Every major brand of Toothpaste has Fluoride, [which causes tooth damage.](http://https://www.google.com/search?q=fluorosis+teeth&hl=en&prmd=imvns&tbm=isch&tbo=u&source=univ&sa=X&ei=sjfBT56-PIqi8QSwpInMCw&ved=0CHQQsAQ&biw=927&bih=477) So all of this is the reason why Dr. Brownstein recommends 50mg iodine for life. If you make every effort to eliminate Halogens from your life, you may be able to cut your iodine dosage to 12.5mg a day. A reverse osmosis filter for your water, fluoride free toothpaste, Bromide free bread, etc. VERY IMPORTANT TO READ ALL LABELS. If the product includes Bro... Flu... Chlo... don't buy it. Even then it is impossible to eliminate all sources. The Toxic Halogens are in drugs, vaccines, hair coloring, furniture, cars... everywhere.

See [this article](http://http://www.freegrab.net/Iodine%20update.htm#section18) for halide toxins. This is a lifetime battle to keep this crap out of our bodies, and also keep our iodine reserves high.

[http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references](http://http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references)

### Grizz — 2012-06-10T20:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-30434>

**One more very important concept described perfectly by Dr. Kruse,

> One clinical finding **I always ask about is the presence of fibrocystic breast disease. When it is present I immediately think the woman is likely suffering from an undiagnosed iodine deficiency. I distinctly remember in medical school seeing a woman’s fibrocystic disease cured in less than an hour by an ancient physician who taught us how to examine a women’s breasts. She had volunteered to be a guinea pig for us during our medical education, and on this day we could not examine her without making her scream and cry because her breasts were so tender to palpation. Our MD proctor was about 80 years old and he too was a volunteer helping us learn our craft. He stopped me from examining her breasts and asked her a few questions and then he asked us to leave the room. He applied Lugol’s solution to her vulva and her nipples and we were able to examine her without any pain a half hour later.** It made a huge impression on me. He asked her if she was on any thyroid meds and if she had bad energy or weight gain. A lack of iodine was the source of her painful breasts.

**

> Her labs did not reveal any major issues when we saw her. I never forgot this lesson on the thyroid.
>
> [WHAT ARE THE TOP TEN PALEO SUPPLEMENTS?](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-are-the-top-ten-paleo-supplements/) \- Jack Kruse

In addition to taking iodine internally, to help speed up the healing process, you can paint iodine over the ovaries and paint the vulva to expedite more iodine directly to the ovaries. However use a carrier oil with the iodine because if iodine is applied repeatedly to the same skin spots every day, it will irritate the skin and result in dermatitis. Carrier oils to use include grapeseed oil, olive oil, etc.

The men suffering from Prostate Problems, desensitized Glans, erectile dysfunction & problems with ejaculate have found rapid improvement from painting iodine on the testicles.

### Grizz — 2012-06-11T19:08:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-30543>

> **Souldanzer;30638 wrote: What's the dilution for the carrier oil... I've been using lugol's topically - no irritation this far.**

**

@@Souldanzer,

If you apply lugols to the same spot every day your skin will eventually develop dermatitis. Some people get around this by painting alternate locations. Example left nipple one day, right nipple the next day. Or painting over the Left Ovary one day and over the right ovary the next day. Placing a few drops of a carrier oil such as Grapeseed Oil or Olive Oil into the palm of your hand and mixing in a few drops of lugols, then rub the mixture over your desired painting area will prevent the dermatitus. I have used a small squeeze bottle to mix Grapeseed oil & Lugols, shake well before painting. Mix ratio is not an exact science. About 50/50 ratio seems to work well.

Do not use a carrier oil to paint skin lesions, or moles.** The lugols should irritate those conditions in order to eliminate them.

@@Paleocowgirl,

What is your iodine daily dosage now? Dr. Brownstein will want you to titrate up to more than 50mg with the required supplements for PCOS.

Details here:

[http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references](http://http://tinyurl.com/iodine-references)

PCOS is treated successfully with iodine, see the testimonials in the above file. I can't discuss what your doctor wants to do. Ask your doctor to help you with iodine supplementation. If he won't there are plenty of iodine doctors that will:

[http://breastcancerchoices.org/ipractitioners.html](http://http://breastcancerchoices.org/ipractitioners.html)

How often? I don't have any references on that question yet. If it were me, I would paint every day, and in fact I do paint my testicles every day for manly problems. Also consider painting over the ovary location.

See these links:

Iodine and PCOS

(33) [http://www.optimox.com/pics/Iodine/pdfs/IOD02.pdf](http://http://www.optimox.com/pics/Iodine/pdfs/IOD02.pdf)

Orthoiodosupplementation: Iodine sufficiency of the whole human Guy. E. Abraham M.D.1, Jorge D. Flechas M.D.2 and John C. Hakala R.Ph.Our preliminary experience with I supplementation at 12.5 mg/day. Our findings in 3 patients with Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome (PCOS) confirmed the positive response observed following supplementation with 10 to 20 mg of potassium iodide by Russian investigators 40 years ago (62). Prior to I supplementation, those PCOS patients were olygomenorrheic, menstruating one or twice a year. Following I supplementation for 3 months, they resumed normal monthly cycles.

(34) [http://optimox.com/pics/Iodine/opt\_Research\_I.shtml](http://http://optimox.com/pics/Iodine/opt_Research_I.shtml)

Listing of Iodine publications at the Optimox Web Site.

(35) [http://optimox.com/pics/Iodine/IOD-10/IOD\_10.htm](http://http://optimox.com/pics/Iodine/IOD-10/IOD_10.htm)

Orthoiodosupplementation in a Primary Care Practice Jorge D. Flechas, M.D.

Iodine deficiency may cause the ovaries to develop cysts , nodules and scar tissue. At its worse this ovarian pathology is very similar to that of polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS). As of the writing of this article I have five PCOS patients. The patients have successfully been brought under control with the use of 50 mg of iodine per day. Control with these patients meaning cysts are gone, periods every 28 days and type 2 diabetes mellitus under control.

(36) [http://cypress.he.net/~bigmacnc/drflechas/index.htm](http://http://cypress.he.net/~bigmacnc/drflechas/index.htm)

HelpMyThyroid, George Flechas MD web site

All from this excellent post at Curezone:

[http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=1586646#i](http://http://curezone.com/forums/fm.asp?i=1586646#i)

### Grizz — 2012-06-11T23:53:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-30606>

Dr. Michael B. Schachter says, “The treatment dose when a person is iodine insufficient is generally between 12.5 mg and 50 mg daily. Preliminary research indicates that if a person is iodine insufficient, it takes about 3 months to become iodine sufficient while ingesting a dosage of 50 mg of iodine and a year to become iodine sufficient while ingesting a dosage of 12.5 mg of iodine daily. However, the patient needs to be monitored closely with awareness of possible side effects and detoxification reactions.” This is quite a bit of iodine and if his statements can be substantiated then most people are using dosages which are much too low.

Rest of the article here:

[http://iodine.imva.info/index.php/iodine-dosages/](http://http://iodine.imva.info/index.php/iodine-dosages/)

Edit: The Iodine Doctors don't recommend Nascent Iodine, they only recommend Lugols or iodoral because they have both iodine & iodide in the correct ratios.

Grizz

### Grizz — 2012-06-13T21:21:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-30951>

I just ran into this tip for PCOS in my travels: Since it is from the highly respected Dr. Dach, I thought it was worthwhile to post it here:

> The addition of progesterone cream has proven beneficial to many women suffering with PCOS. The information below is derived from ([http://jeffreydach.com/2008/02/13/understanding-pcos-the-hidden-epidemic-by-jeffrey-dach-md.aspx](http://http://jeffreydach.com/2008/02/13/understanding-pcos-the-hidden-epidemic-by-jeffrey-dach-md.aspx))
>
>
>
> Guidelines for Progesterone Cream Dosage for PCOS (8)
>
>
>
> Early PCOS - 32mg from day 12-26
>
>
>
> Advanced PCOS - 54mg from day 12-26 of your cycle
>
>
>
> Severe PCOS with pain, 64mg of progesterone cream from day 5-26 , to address pain from endometriosis. Then try to wean back to a lesser dose or to extend breaks to fall into line with a day 12-26 cycle. Note, if you are using a regime day 5-26 in the first 4-7 months until symptoms settle, please be aware you are using a program suggested to enhance fertility. (8)
>
>
>
>
>
> Dr. Lam Progesterone Guidelines for Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (9)
>
>
>
> Dr. Lam follows Dr John R Lee pioneering use of progesterone.
>
>
>
> Apply 20 mg of progesterone cream during day 14 to 28 of the menstrual cycle. Adjust accordingly if for longer or shorter cycle. As the hormonal balance is regained, facial hair and acne, two commonly associated symptoms, will disappear. (9)
>
> [http://theiodineproject.webs.com/imdeficientnowwhat.htm](http://http://theiodineproject.webs.com/imdeficientnowwhat.htm)

Has anyone tried this? Comments?

Grizz

I'm on progesterone cream but have no cycle, so I take daily, morning and night. I just bought a cream with progesterone, pregnelone and 7-Keto DHEA in it, so I will see how that goes.

On another note, those of you struggling with PCOS, how do you handle cravings? It seems for the longest time, BAB with lots of fat killed those cravings, then I had to do a glucose reading (i.e. ingest sugar) for my doc to diagnose LR and PCOS, and ever since, I have had horrible cravings and have had a hard time not binging, even though I eat a BAB each morning. This follows with restrictive eating, and I'm afraid is leading to an unhealthy cycle of an ED. Help? Thoughts?

I just had a lengthy conversation with Dr Kalish today about neurotransmitters and eating behavior/EDs... you might want to check out his work. He actually works together with an ED therapist and her clients. He's convinced that much disordered eating is based in neurotransmitter dysfunctions.... especially bad if you've had problems for a long time and they're not resolving on a clean diet.

I almost want to curse about your friend...I'll refrain. I am in the SAME situation (re: people that can 'eat what they want and still stay skinny and these are MENOPAUSAL women too...thought you're supposed to gain weight...? I bet that will only be me :-() I always feel alone now. It sucks and I want to scream about un-fairness.

It's true that even not worrying about 'fat' that I would still want to eat healthy, b/c I DO feel way better. But to be honest, I feel like I am negatively impacted now (July)...where it is affecting my social life. For example, I declined TWO things b/c I was soooooo freaking afraid of having to eat \*\*\*\* (and not the \*\*\*\*...but getting fat from them). I HATE it....

Yes, I used to be stupidly afraid of the cold. I think CT has REALLY helped me there...so at least that's a good thing.

Sigh. I feel bad too right now. (But hopefully you're feeling better now).

> **August;22689 wrote:** Source: CT-12 GETTING BACK â€œON BOARDâ€￾ WITH MY MESSAGE forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?877-The-PCOS-Support-Thread!/page3
>
>

>

>

> I guess specifically I am thinking of this whole food sensitivity thing . . . If I understand correctly Dean Dwyer seems to say in his book that eating certain foods makes you fat (and that what those foods are is an individual thing) . . . I might infer that that could refer to food sensitivities . . although it could also have more to do with maco-nurtion (carb/fat/protien) content . . . in any case this whole business of avoiding all the foods on my sensitivity causing list does not seem sustainable to me (plus I don't notice any benefit . . but it might be too soon to tell) . . . and I guess if there was a huge benefit . . that would be a horse of a different color . . I might decide that it was sustainable (I guess for me this sustainability issues seems like of subjective . . or maybe I am making it subjective . . according to my whelms) . . . The book also says:
>
> Dwyer, Dean (2012-04-11). Make Shift Happen: Change How You Look by Changing How You Think (Kindle Locations 535-536). Victory Belt Publishing. Kindle Edition. . . . as someone with PCOS . . who still wants to be lean . . I don't exactly know what to do with that statement. . . . I guess the things that the book talks about are things that I ponder . . a lot (I'm only about half way through the book) . . I guess the whole thing about certain foods are making you \[me\] fat . . and the thing about sustainability sort of has me in a quandary . .
>
>
>
> Oh I was thinking about my McDonnalds munching naturally thin friend . . she seem to be way better cold adapted than I am . . . I actually went home angry because I was so miserable and physically uncomfortable staying there . .

### Grizz — 2012-07-24T21:02:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-40778>

> Originally Posted by Grizz
>
> Here is the deal... Dr. Brownstein recommends higher levels (than 50mg) of iodine when a person has a disease. You might want to research how high to go. Some ladies in our group have gone as high as 100mg. I have documented a woman's case history in the Iodine References, chapter "Aggressive Iodine Dosing Notes." You might want to find an iodine doctor to help you with this. Here is the list of Iodine Doctors:
>
> [http://breastcancerchoices.org/ipractitioners.html](http://http://breastcancerchoices.org/ipractitioners.html)
>
>
>
>
> **August;30725 wrote:** I thought 50 mg was the crazy high dose for getting levels back up . . not the maintenance dose . . I thought around 12mg was about what what a Japanese person might get per day (more seafood/sea vegeatble intake in Japan) . . and therefore might be a good maintenance dose . . 12mg is still a LOT higher than the US RDA . .which I believe is ridiculously low . . . I'm still trying to adjust my thinking to include the possibility of higher dosing . and also higher levels for maintenance (once the deficiency no longer exists) . . . aren't there some concerns when the dosage for idoine goes higher . . like too rapid detoxing . . or being hard on the organs involved in detox/elimination . . for some reason iodine supplementation always makes me feel a little worried re: kidney health . . I don't know if that's valid or not . .. maybe I'm worrying about the wrong thing . . .

August,

You are correct. Dr. Brownstein recommends 50mg as our maintenance dose in order to overcome the amount of Halogen Toxins entering our body everyday. I am currently on 150mg a day & near the end of my detox cycle.

I plan to drop down to 25mg daily for maintenance because:

\* My drinking water is filtered from a well and has no chlorine or Fluorides

\* I use a fluoride free toothpaste

\* We don't eat bread with bromides

\* We are on a strict Paleo Diet and we read labels.

Not to Worry. Dr. Brownstein, the [Yahoo Iodine Group](http://http://health.groups.yahoo.com/group/iodine/), [BreastCancerChoices,](http://http://breastcancerchoices.org/ipractitioners.html) and [Curezone](http://http://curezone.com/forums/f.asp?f=815) have all proven 100++mg of iodine to be perfectly safe if taken with the required co-supplements:

\- - Â½ tsp Natural Celtic Salt, Himalayan Pink Salt, Redmonds Salt or Hawaiin black salt

\- - - If the sea salt is pure white, it is unacceptable (minerals have been stripped out )

\- - 200 mcg selenium (L-selenomethionine preferred)

\- - 400 mg Magnesium - Glycinate preferred

\- - 2,000mg Vitamin C - Ester-C preferred - do not take with iodine

\- - ATP Cofactors caplet contains both 100mg B2 & 500mg B3 non-flushing in the correct ratio. Take 1/2 caplet with iodine up to 25mg, then one caplet for each 50 mg iodine,

Grizz

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-25T16:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pcos-support-thread.833/post-40986>

So - today I pick up my cycloset (Like Jack predicted - my Pharmacy had to special order it!) so I'll be taking my 1st dose tomorrow morning when I wake up... getting my butt outside for some AM Light therapy, strapping on some Ice packs for some spot CT - and testing my FBG before sitting down to a BAB.

Hoping the cycloset will not only reset my circadian cycles, but that it will also lower my FBG - if not we'll be looking at adding Metaformin (Which my PCP doesn't like to use - b/c she hasn't had a lot of success w/it for her patients - not sure if that's b/c they're not eating a clean low carb diet or something else)

Still working on the T and E-Dominance, and Low Pg. I know I need more Pg as I had a migraine from Hell yesterday - cycle day 24 - like clock work - for the past 3 cycles day 24 migraine - and TOTM on day 26 - so I expect TOTM to show up tomorrow! after talking w/Colleen - I'm changing the application location of the Pg - b/c it seems like I'm not absorbing it.... Jack may be right that I need additional Pg in the form of 200mg of prometrium - PCP wants me to see how changing the application site works and stay w/Dzugan's recommendations for now - We'll retests in September - a full set of labs... and go from there....

Shijin, this makes sense to me. I have been puzzled why my FBG went up from 90's to high 100's. I recently added thyroid medication and started cycling my estrogen and progesterone a month ago. I also increased the dosage of progesterone and added DHEA 12.5 mg a couple of weeks ago. Once I was going down with progesterone, my sugar shot up between 106-113. I don't have PCOS but there must be connection with the dosing of progesterone or DHEA. I will confirm once I go up with my progesterone dose in 10 days.

I am waiting for Metformin and hoping that it will bring my FBG down.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-12T20:15:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-want-everyone-to-know-that-with-spring-and-summer-coming-in-northern-hemisphere.834/post-14312>

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-12T20:49:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-want-everyone-to-know-that-with-spring-and-summer-coming-in-northern-hemisphere.834/post-14331>

### Croak — 2012-04-12T21:00:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-want-everyone-to-know-that-with-spring-and-summer-coming-in-northern-hemisphere.834/post-14333>

### shsatori — 2013-04-29T19:30:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-want-everyone-to-know-that-with-spring-and-summer-coming-in-northern-hemisphere.834/post-85149>

**Nutiva coconut manna recipe**

Hi Dr. Jack, I have just joined your forum but have been studying your website and practicing your form of Paleo for over 8 weeks now and I am feeling like a new person. Thank you only begins to express how I feel. I love Nutiva coconut manna and I wanted to put in this recipe to win your new cookbook. I just hope this is the right place for you or your team to see it today! Here goes--Kruzin coconut custard --4 organic eggs, 2-3 teaspoons Nutiva coconut manna, 1 Tab. arrowroot flour, 1/2 teasp. vanilla, stevia to taste(optional) Put 3/4 cup coconut milk in the top of a double boiler. Fill bottom pan of boiler with 3-4 in. water. Start heating. Beat 4 eggs in a bowl. Add arrowroot flour that is completly mixed in a portion of 1/4 cup of the remaining milk. Add this and remainder of milk to beaten eggs and use a wisk to beat all together. Add this to heated milk slowly while wisking and also add 2-3 teasp. of the manna. Wisk until smooth and thick enough for a custard consistency and then remove from heat. Add vanilla and stir and stevia to taste(if you wish). Enjoy!This is a Spring recipe

### nonchalant — 2013-04-29T21:10:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-want-everyone-to-know-that-with-spring-and-summer-coming-in-northern-hemisphere.834/post-85151>

Hi Shsatori, welcome!
You might want to post this in the Community Cookbook forum.
[http://jackkruse.com/forum/forumdisplay.php?36-Community-Cookbook](http://jackkruse.com/forum/forumdisplay.php?36-Community-Cookbook)

Looks delicious!

# Thread: Jack Kruse is Vincenz Priessnitz Re-incarnated!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-is-vincenz-priessnitz-re-incarnated.840/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-12T22:00:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kruse-is-vincenz-priessnitz-re-incarnated.840/post-14364>

From: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vincent\_Priessnitz](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vincent_Priessnitz)

Vincenz Priessnitz was born into a farmer's family in the village of GrÃ¤fenberg. Once Vinzenz watched a roebuck with a wounded limb coming to a pond (or stream) to heal its wound. He healed his own finger injured during timber felling with water wraps (1814). In 1816 he was injured more seriously when he broke his ribs in an accident with a cart and the doctor claimed it was fatal or at least crippling. He used his water therapy which took a year but eventually succeeded. He became locally renowned and started healing animals and some neighbours by pouring cold water. Soon queues of people were coming to GrÃ¤fenberg, so in 1822 Vincenz decided to rebuild his father's house, building part of it as a sanatorium for his patients.

In 1826 he was invited to Vienna to heal the EmperorÂ´s brother Anton Victor, Grand Master of the Teutonic Knights. It made him a great reputation so a lot of people from all over the country started to stream to GrÃ¤fenberg. Among the methods he used in his therapy there was not only cold water but also hard work and barefoot walks on grass. The diet and sleep regime were quite strict. His â€œsponge washingâ€￾ was not accepted by local doctors of medicine, and so they complained and called him an impostor with no medical background. The complaints were denied by the imperial committee which accepted hydrotherapy as an alternative medical method and in 1838 granted Priessnitz a permit to establish the spa he started several years earlier.

The next year 1500 patients arrived (among them one monarch, a duke and duchess, 22 princes and 149 counts and countesses) and 120 doctors to study the new therapy. A visit by Arch-Duke Franz Carl in October 1845 was greeted with an address extolling the virtues of Priessnitz and his methods, signed by 124 guests, from a variety of countries.

# Thread: nothing succeeds like success
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-04-13T00:51:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-14457>

I went to the doc today for a follow-up on my foot. I have been CT'ing my foot, and the inflammation is way down. There is a touch of redness, but now I have defined borders, and can walk comfortably. I asked him about CT, and he agreed with my other (Ukrainian) doctor, CT full speed ahead. But, he was curious, why would I be reluctant to do CT? I told him others thought it was controversial. He said, "well, what's controversial? Look how much better your foot is." This morning I did my first completely freezing shower, with freezing morning water. It was a shock for a few seconds, then easy, and I didn't shiver at all. My hands and feet were still very cold at the Dr's office. Next time I go back to the doc, he wants me after a day on my feet, because after the shower, it looked pretty good.

### Birdy — 2012-04-13T00:57:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-14458>

I think what is going on with these docs, is that they are being graded on their success, so when they see one, they are very interested in hearing about it and applying it to other patients.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-16T00:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nothing-succeeds-like-success.847/post-15405>
